title: Phase Field Simulation for Dendrite Growth in Energy Storage Batteries

MaterialSystem.evidence: In this paper the phase transition means the transformation from the electrolyte phase to the electrode phase, as shown in Fig. 1b.

Phenomenon.description: Growth and morphological evolution of metal dendrites at the electrode-electrolyte interface in energy storage batteries.
Phenomenon.evidence: The model can not only accurately simulate the dynamic changes of the interface but also reflect the influence of external conditions such as temperature, electric field, and stress on the dendrite morphology and growth rate.

Assumption.statement: The electrode-electrolyte interface is treated as a transition region rather than a sharp interface.
Assumption.evidence: During dendrite growth, the electrode–electrolyte interface (EEI) surface does not exhibit a simply sharp interface but rather features a distinct transition region.

Assumption.statement: Phases are regarded as non-conserved variables whose evolution follows the Allen-Cahn equation.
Assumption.evidence: Phases are usually regarded as non-conserved variables, and their evolution follows the Allen-Cahn equation:

Assumption.statement: If there is no chemical reaction, concentration variables are conserved and follow the Cahn-Hilliard equation.
Assumption.evidence: If there is no chemical reaction, the concentration variable are conserved variables and follows the Cahn-Hilliard equation:

Assumption.statement: The phase-field variable is used as the central descriptor and coupled with ion concentration, electric potential, temperature, stress, SEI, and anisotropy.
Assumption.evidence: Therefore, it can be concluded that the phase-field model uses the phase-field variable as the central descriptor, while coupling features such as ion concentration and electric potential, thereby constructing an extensible modeling framework capable of comprehensively accounting for multiple influencing factors.

SimulationModel.objective: Describe and analyze dendrite growth by coupling phase evolution with ion concentration, electric field, temperature, stress, SEI, and anisotropy.
SimulationModel.evidence: In theory, the phase-field method constructs a free-energy functional and describes dendritic growth by solving time-dependent evolution equations based on the variational derivative of that functional.

ModelEquation.description: Evolution equation for non-conserved phase variables describing smooth transition between phases.
ModelEquation.evidence: Phases are usually regarded as non-conserved variables, and their evolution follows the Allen-Cahn equation:

ModelEquation.description: Evolution equation for conserved concentration variables of chemical species.
ModelEquation.evidence: If there is no chemical reaction, the concentration variable are conserved variables and follows the Cahn-Hilliard equation:

ModelEquation.description: Total free energy functional composed of chemical, electrostatic, elastic, and other coupled energy terms.
ModelEquation.evidence: To solve the above equations, the total free energy of the system is expressed as an integral of energy terms, as shown in Eq. 3. These energy terms are functions of the defined field variables, such as chemical free energy, elastic strain energy, and electrostatic energy.

Term.description: Mobility term governing interface evolution between phases.
Term.evidence: In the formula, Lαβ represents the interface mobility between phase α and phase β

Term.description: Variational derivative of free energy with respect to the phase field.
Term.evidence: In the formula, Lαβ represents the interface mobility between phase α and phase β, and f represents the free energy density.

Term.description: Mobility term for transport between components i and j.
Term.evidence: In the formula, Mij is the atomic mobility between component i and component j.

Term.description: Chemical-potential-gradient-driven transport contribution in the concentration evolution equation.
Term.evidence: If there is no chemical reaction, the concentration variable are conserved variables and follows the Cahn-Hilliard equation:

Term.description: Chemical free energy contribution to the total free energy.
Term.evidence: In the formula, fchem represents chemical energy

Term.description: Electrostatic energy contribution to the total free energy.
Term.evidence: In the formula, felec represents electrostatic energy

Term.description: Elastic strain energy contribution to the total free energy.
Term.evidence: In the formula, felas represents elastic energy.

Parameter.description: Field variable describing the phase state of the system.
Parameter.evidence: In the phase field model, the basic field variables are the phase field variable ξα (α = 1,…,n), which describe the phase states of the system.

Parameter.description: Interface mobility between phase α and phase β.
Parameter.evidence: In the formula, Lαβ represents the interface mobility between phase α and phase β

Parameter.description: Free energy density used in the governing equations.
Parameter.evidence: In the formula, Lαβ represents the interface mobility between phase α and phase β, and f represents the free energy density.

Parameter.description: Concentration variable of chemical components in the system.
Parameter.evidence: Therefore, to accurately calculate the free energy function of the system, the concentration variables ci(I = 1,…,m) are also introduced into the system.

Parameter.description: Atomic mobility between component i and component j.
Parameter.evidence: In the formula, Mij is the atomic mobility between component i and component j.

Parameter.description: Total free energy of the system.
Parameter.evidence: To solve the above equations, the total free energy of the system is expressed as an integral of energy terms

Parameter.description: Chemical energy density contribution to total free energy.
Parameter.evidence: In the formula, fchem represents chemical energy

Parameter.description: Electrostatic energy density contribution to total free energy.
Parameter.evidence: In the formula, felec represents electrostatic energy

Parameter.description: Elastic energy density contribution to total free energy.
Parameter.evidence: In the formula, felas represents elastic energy.

Parameter.description: Applied voltage or electric-field driving condition affecting dendrite morphology.
Parameter.evidence: As the driving force for dendrite growth, the electric field leads to a morphological evolution from mossy structures (initially also needle-like or fibrous) to tree-like structures with increased branching as the applied voltage increases.

Parameter.description: Lithium-ion concentration, especially near the anode, influencing dendrite evolution.
Parameter.evidence: This phenomenon is attributed to the depletion of lithium ion concentration near the anode under high applied voltage.

Parameter.description: System temperature affecting dendrite morphology.
Parameter.evidence: With increasing temperature, the dendrite morphology evolves from tip-splitting tree-like to fibrous.

Parameter.description: Temperature gradient affecting dendrite morphology.
Parameter.evidence: In addition, a higher temperature gradient causes the morphology to shift from tree-like to spherical forms.

Parameter.description: Applied or internal stress affecting dendrite trunk and branching morphology.
Parameter.evidence: The application of stress results in a thinner main trunk and a significant reduction in side branches, even leading to fracture of the main trunk.

Parameter.description: Anisotropy incorporated into free energy formulation to capture directional effects on dendrite morphology.
Parameter.evidence: An increase in anisotropy gives rise to more nucleation sites and main trunks, causing the morphology to change from mossy to tree-like or needle-like structures.

Parameter.description: Granular character of the solid electrolyte interphase affecting dendrite morphology.
Parameter.evidence: The granular nature of the SEI can induce a transition from filamentous to bush-like dendrite structures.

Parameter.description: Degree of side branching in dendrite morphology.
Parameter.evidence: As the driving force for dendrite growth, the electric field leads to a morphological evolution from mossy structures (initially also needle-like or fibrous) to tree-like structures with increased branching as the applied voltage increases.

Parameter.description: Thickness of the main dendrite trunk.
Parameter.evidence: The application of stress results in a thinner main trunk

Parameter.description: Number of nucleation sites during dendrite formation.
Parameter.evidence: An increase in anisotropy gives rise to more nucleation sites and main trunks

Parameter.description: Number of main trunks in dendrite morphology.
Parameter.evidence: An increase in anisotropy gives rise to more nucleation sites and main trunks

Condition.evidence: This phenomenon is attributed to the depletion of lithium ion concentration near the anode under high applied voltage.
With increasing temperature, the dendrite morphology evolves from tip-splitting tree-like to fibrous.
In addition, a higher temperature gradient causes the morphology to shift from tree-like to spherical forms.
The application of stress results in a thinner main trunk and a significant reduction in side branches, even leading to fracture of the main trunk.
An increase in anisotropy gives rise to more nucleation sites and main trunks, causing the morphology to change from mossy to tree-like or needle-like structures.
The granular nature of the SEI can induce a transition from filamentous to bush-like dendrite structures.

Effect.evidence: This phenomenon is attributed to the depletion of lithium ion concentration near the anode under high applied voltage.

As the driving force for dendrite growth, the electric field leads to a morphological evolution from mossy structures (initially also needle-like or fibrous) to tree-like structures with increased branching as the applied voltage increases.
The granular nature of the SEI can induce a transition from filamentous to bush-like dendrite structures.
With increasing temperature, the dendrite morphology evolves from tip-splitting tree-like to fibrous.
In addition, a higher temperature gradient causes the morphology to shift from tree-like to spherical forms.
The application of stress results in a thinner main trunk

An increase in anisotropy gives rise to more nucleation sites and main trunks
